museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: ColecoVision expands, Donkey Kong Jr. arrives in arcades, the Dragon 32 launches in Britain, and the Commodore 64 begins its public retail life.
museum.curatorNote: August is one of the richest 1982 hardware months. Some release records are month-level, so the exhibit avoids invented days unless sources support them.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: August turns hardware competition into something visible.

items.title: ColecoVision expands in North America
items.body: After limited late-July rollout, ColecoVision expanded to more US markets with Donkey Kong as the conversion everyone wanted to compare.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive describes late-July launch followed by August expansion.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr. releases in arcades
items.body: Nintendo's sequel inverted the first game's rescue story and became one of 1982's major arcade follow-ups.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames, Donkey Kong Wiki and Arcade History list August 1982.

items.title: Dragon 32 launches in the UK
items.body: The Welsh-built Dragon 32 entered Britain's home-computer market as another serious colour micro for families and schools to consider.

items.title: Commodore 64 marketing begins
items.body: After its January introduction, the C64 entered wider public marketing and retail life in August, carrying a strong games promise through its sound and graphics hardware.
items.sourceNote: C64 histories list marketing from August 1982.

items.title: Home arcade becomes a hardware battle
items.body: ColecoVision, Atari and Intellivision now looked less like interchangeable boxes and more like competing claims about how close home could get to the arcade.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: August has real arrivals: console rollout, arcade sequel, and British micro hardware.

items.title: ColecoVision
items.body: Wider North American market expansion with Donkey Kong as defining pack-in.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: The pack-in conversion that sold the machine's arcade promise.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr.
items.body: Nintendo's arcade sequel gives 1982 another character platformer anchor.

items.title: Dragon 32
items.body: A UK home-computer release with games potential and school/family positioning.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: Public marketing/retail window for a machine whose games legacy would become enormous.

items.title: BurgerTime
items.body: Data East's food-stacking arcade game belongs to 1982's character-action wave, though exact regional months vary by source.
items.sourceNote: Included as 1982 arcade context, not a precise August claim.

items.title: Moon Patrol
items.body: Irem's side-scrolling moon buggy shooter is another 1982 arcade landmark often discussed around mid-year release windows.

items.title: Spectrum cassette releases
items.body: The UK cassette software stream kept growing around the young machine.
items.sourceNote: Specific August title dates not asserted.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: August is a hardware display case.

items.title: ColecoVision
items.body: Its Donkey Kong pack-in made the system feel like a serious home-arcade challenger.

items.title: Dragon 32
items.body: The Dragon joined the UK home-computer shelf with a proper keyboard and colour graphics, aiming at homes and schools.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: The C64's SID sound and sprite hardware made it feel unusually ready for games.

items.title: Atari 2600 comparison point
items.body: The old leader now had to be compared against newer machines claiming better arcade fidelity.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: August issues had new boxes to diagram.

items.title: ColecoVision coverage
items.headline: Arcade quality comes home
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for August 1982.
items.curatorNote: The console's pitch was made for magazine comparison photos.
items.body: The console's pitch was made for magazine comparison photos.

items.title: Dragon and Spectrum listings
items.headline: New British micros on the shelf
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for August 1982.
items.curatorNote: The weekly UK press made new machines feel like a living marketplace.
items.body: The weekly UK press made new machines feel like a living marketplace.

items.title: C&VG arcade sequel context
items.headline: Donkey Kong Jr. and the new home challengers
items.curatorNote: Arcade sequel and console competition were now one story.
items.body: Arcade sequel and console competition were now one story.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Choosing between serious home computers
items.curatorNote: For families, choosing a micro still sounded like buying educational equipment, even when games were waiting.
items.body: For families, choosing a micro still sounded like buying educational equipment, even when games were waiting.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Comparison was the work of magazines and shops.

items.title: Screenshots were evidence
items.body: Magazine comparison shots mattered because most readers could not try every machine.

items.title: Shop demos were rare gateways
items.body: Seeing Donkey Kong on ColecoVision could make the hardware argument instantly.

items.title: Listings and adverts moved software
items.body: For micro owners, discovery was still printed and postal.

items.title: Arcade knowledge stayed social
items.body: Donkey Kong Jr. strategy spread through watching and retelling.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: August made 1982 feel crowded with machines.

items.title: The home arcade promise improved
items.body: ColecoVision made players believe the gap might shrink.

items.title: British computers multiplied
items.body: Spectrum, Dragon, BBC and Commodore made the UK shelf feel wonderfully unresolved.

items.title: Arcades still led character design
items.body: Donkey Kong Jr. proved the cabinet could still introduce personalities faster than home systems.

items.title: The future had too many plugs
items.body: Consoles, computers, cassette decks and TVs all wanted space.

sources.label: ColecoVision launch chronology
sources.note: Used for late July/August rollout and Donkey Kong pack-in.

sources.label: Donkey Kong Jr. MobyGames release
sources.note: Used for August 1982 arcade release month.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft; needs human editorial review and source verification.
